title: Analyzing the two-dimensional doped Hubbard model with the Worldvolume HMC method
abstract: We apply the Worldvolume Hybrid Monte Carlo (WV-HMC) method [arXiv:2012.08468] to the two-dimensional Hubbard model, which is known to suffer from a severe sign problem when the system is doped (away from half filling). We show that the method predicts physical observables with controlled statistical errors on an $8 \times 8$ lattice at temperature $T/t = 1/6.4 \approx 0.156$ and interaction strength $U/t = 8.0$ ($t$ is the hopping amplitude), for which the standard determinant quantum Monte Carlo fails.
summary: This paper presents the application of the Worldvolume Hybrid Monte Carlo method to solve the sign problem in the doped 2D Hubbard model. It demonstrates that this approach can accurately calculate physical observables in regimes where standard determinant QMC methods fail. The results are validated by showing convergence to known results in the continuum limit.
main_problem: The numerical sign problem prevents standard determinant quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) from simulating the 2D Hubbard model when it is doped away from half-filling.
main_result: The Worldvolume Hybrid Monte Carlo (WV-HMC) method successfully predicts physical observables with controlled statistical errors in the doped regime where standard methods fail.
method: The Worldvolume Hybrid Monte Carlo (WV-HMC) method, which uses an anti-holomorphic flow to deform the integration surface into a continuum union of surfaces (the worldvolume) to mitigate the sign problem.
model_or_system: A two-dimensional Hubbard model on a periodic, bipartite square lattice, characterized by hopping amplitude (t), on-site repulsion (U), and chemical potential (mu).
key_observables: Number density operator <n> and energy density operator <e>.
important_parameters: Lattice size 8x8, temperature T/t = 1/6.4, interaction strength U/t = 8.0, and the tuning parameter alpha for balancing sign suppression and ergodicity.
main_assumptions: Uses a symmetric Trotter decomposition up to O(epsilon^2) and assumes the validity of the continuum limit as the Trotter step epsilon approaches zero.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares number density against ALF results; Figure 2 shows finite-epsilon effects on number density; Figure 3 presents number density and energy density in the continuum limit.
paper_structure: The paper defines the sign problem, introduces the WV-HMC method, details the Hubbard model Hamiltonian and path integral implementation, presents simulation results for density and energy, and discusses the continuum limit and ergodicity.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: The number density ⟨𝑛⟩at 𝜖= 0.27, 0.29, 0.32, compared with results using ALF at 𝜖= 0.01.
Figure 2: Finite-𝜖effects in the number density ⟨𝑛⟩for ˜𝜇= 1.0, 3.0, 5.0, 7.0 (figure adapted from [25]).
Figure 3: Number density ⟨𝑛⟩and energy density ⟨𝑒⟩in the continuum limit 𝜖→0, compared with results using ALF at 𝜖= 0.01 (figure adapted from [25]).
